attributes.stem: A 65-year-old man is brought to the Emergency Department following a sudden collapse. Despite resuscitation attempts, he is pronounced dead. A post-mortem examination is conducted. The pathologist examining sections of the coronary arteries notes raised lesions within the arterial walls.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the typical composition of an atheromatous plaque?
options: Soft lipid core covered by a fibrous cap
Dense lipid deposits with concentric fibrous rings
Lipid-laden macrophages surrounded by inflammatory cells
Lipid crystals embedded within calcified matrix
Extracellular lipid pools with smooth muscle proliferation
attributes.explanation:
An atheromatous plaque is a characteristic lesion of atherosclerosis, a progressive disease affecting medium and large arteries. The typical atheromatous plaque consists of a soft lipid core covered by a fibrous cap. The lipid core (also called the necrotic core) contains cholesterol, cholesterol esters, and cellular debris. The fibrous cap is composed primarily of smooth muscle cells, collagen, and proteoglycans.

Dense lipid deposits with concentric fibrous rings is not an accurate description of the typical atheromatous plaque structure. While lipids are certainly present in atheromatous plaques, they typically form a central core rather than appearing as dense deposits arranged in concentric rings with fibrous tissue.

Lipid-laden macrophages surrounded by inflammatory cells describes foam cells, which are indeed present in atherosclerotic lesions, particularly in early fatty streaks and within the lipid core of advanced plaques. However, this description focuses on just one component rather than capturing the overall structure of an atheromatous plaque with its characteristic fibrous cap covering a lipid core.

Lipid crystals embedded within calcified matrix describes a feature that may be seen in advanced, complicated plaques but is not the typical composition of an atheromatous plaque. Calcification can occur in atherosclerotic plaques, particularly in advanced stages, but the defining feature of an atheromatous plaque is the lipid core covered by a fibrous cap.

Extracellular lipid pools with smooth muscle proliferation captures some elements found in atheromatous plaques but does not accurately describe the typical structure. While extracellular lipids and smooth muscle cells are components of atherosclerotic plaques, the characteristic organization with a distinct lipid core covered by a fibrous cap is not conveyed by this description.

The stability of an atheromatous plaque is largely determined by the thickness and integrity of its fibrous cap. Plaques with thin fibrous caps and large lipid cores are more vulnerable to rupture, which can lead to thrombosis and acute vascular events such as myocardial infarction or stroke. Factors that contribute to plaque instability include inflammation, matrix metalloproteinase activity, and mechanical stress.
learningPoints: Atheromatous plaques consist of a lipid-rich core covered by a fibrous cap.
Plaque stability is determined by the thickness of the fibrous cap and size of the lipid core.
Rupture of the fibrous cap can lead to thrombosis and acute vascular events.
references: Kumar V, Abbas AK, Aster JC. Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease. 10th ed. Philadelphia: Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 11; pages 494
